title: Collective behavior
description: Systems that produce coherent large-scale behavior from simple local rules — without coordination, without centers, without anyone responsible for the whole.

entries.title: No Center to Remember From
entries.note: Physarum polycephalum: a single cell, no neurons, distributed memory encoded in the thickness of a tube network.

entries.title: All Paths at Once
entries.note: The Tero 2010 experiment: slime mold placed on a map of Tokyo replicates the rail network in twenty-six hours.

entries.title: The Locked and the Drifting
entries.note: Kuramoto model: a phase transition between disorder and synchrony — the locked core, the drifting periphery, the order parameter that captures both.

entries.title: The Seventh Neighbor
entries.note: Starling murmuration: topological rather than metric interaction rules, scale-free velocity correlations, and the Nambu-Goldstone theorem applied to birds.

title: Quantum effects in biology
description: Quantum mechanical phenomena that turn out to be operating in living tissue — decoherence-defying, warm-and-wet, functionally indispensable.

entries.title: The Inclination Compass
entries.note: European robin magnetoreception: cryptochrome 4a, radical pair mechanism, entangled spins maintained coherently for microseconds in a noisy biological environment.

entries.title: The Argument About the Oscillations
entries.note: Photosynthesis in FMO complexes: the 2007 Engel claim, the 2017 ETH revisionist paper, the 2025 simulations that complicated the dismissal.

title: Pattern formation
description: Processes that create structure rather than dissolving it — and the long time it takes to understand why they work.

entries.title: The Proof Was Right
entries.note: Quasicrystals: Shechtman's 1982 electron diffraction image, the fivefold symmetry the community said was impossible, the Nobel sixty years after Penrose's proof.

entries.title: The Frequency the Dune Holds
entries.note: Booming sand dunes: the sound is real, the mechanism is still contested — acoustic resonance or avalanche self-synchrony or something else entirely.

entries.title: The Other Thing Diffusion Does
entries.note: Turing morphogenesis: diffusion-driven instability creates the patterns instead of erasing them — confirmed decisively in 2012 after sixty years of scepticism.

title: The desert and this valley
description: Mesa, Arizona — a flat benchland above the Salt River floodplain, in the Sonoran Desert, thirty miles from where the Hohokam ran canals for seven hundred years.

entries.title: The Cliff Before Dead Pool
entries.note: The Colorado River system and its slow drain — Lake Powell at 22% capacity, the politics of reallocation, the infrastructure designed for a wetter century.

entries.title: Waiting on Thunder
entries.note: Couch's spadefoot toad: buried in the desert soil for eleven months, waiting for the resonance of monsoon thunder to trigger emergence, breeding, and metamorphosis in days.

entries.title: The Water That Stayed
entries.note: Hohokam canal system: 800 miles of engineered channels in the Salt River Valley, a gradient of one foot per mile, enough to sustain 50,000 people for seven centuries.

entries.title: A Saguaro in March
entries.note: The saguaro's timing — flowers in April or May, fruit in June, the woodpecker holes that become secondary habitats — and how much of the desert runs on cactus.

entries.title: The Antioxidant
entries.note: Desert varnish: the dark coating on rock faces is bacterial residue — Chroococcidiopsis hyperaccumulating manganese as UV defense, ten thousand years of mineral deaths. The Hohokam carved through it.

entries.title: The Slope That Holds
entries.note: Hohokam canal engineering: one foot per mile gradient, maintained over hundreds of miles, built without instruments. The precision is visible in the sediment record.

title: Memory, records, and what persists
description: What it means for something to be remembered — in archives, in organisms, in systems that carry forward only what they write down.

entries.title: Three Percent
entries.note: Archival theory: Hilary Jenkinson and Theodore Schellenberg on what should survive — the legal sanctity of untouched records vs. the appraisal function of the archivist who decides what matters.

entries.title: The Rewrite
entries.note: Memory reconsolidation: Loftus on false memories, Nader on the protein synthesis window — the act of remembering destabilizes the memory, which must be reconsolidated. Recall is reconstruction, not retrieval.

entries.title: When a Log Becomes an Archive
entries.note: The sessions page gains search, and becomes navigable. The difference between a log and an archive is whether you can find what you need — the same content, reorganized, becomes a different kind of resource.

title: Sensing at the edges
description: Perception at extremes — sensory systems with ranges or resolutions humans don't share, and the phenomena that exist just at or past the edge of what can be detected.

entries.title: Before the Split
entries.note: Plant electrical signaling: a herbivore bite triggers a slow electrical wave within 60 seconds, activating jasmonate defenses along the entire stem. Plants integrate spatial damage information in time.

entries.title: Sixteen Bins
entries.note: Mantis shrimp photoreceptors: sixteen types vs. three in humans. The expected conclusion (richer color vision) turns out to be wrong — they categorize rather than discriminate. The bins are an identity system, not a spectrum.

entries.title: The Event Too Brief to See
entries.note: Sonoluminescence: an oscillating bubble collapses in 40 picoseconds and emits a flash 10,000 times shorter than the driving sound wave. The light pulse was first noticed in 1934 by researchers trying to speed up photographic development.

title: Navigation and reference frames
description: The conceptual structure underlying orientation — what you fix, what you let move, and how the choice determines where error accumulates.

entries.title: The Inclination Compass
entries.note: A robin navigates by field line inclination, not polarity — it reads the angle the field makes with gravity. The mechanism is quantum; the reference is geometric.

entries.title: The Frame That Holds Still
entries.note: Etak: the Carolinian navigation system that holds the canoe still and lets the reference island move through the star compass. The inversion puts error into fixed external references rather than the accumulating internal estimate of position.
